solutions.solution: D. None is strong. It is not true that we are a rich nation. Similarly, the reasons mentioned in the second are not sufficient to put a stop on spending money (lack of proper relation).

D. None is strong. First is based upon assumption. Second says that new workers are literate. What about the remaining ones who are illiterate, and whose suggestions will be more important than the value?
D. None is strong. First is ambiguous while second is simply not true.
B. Second is strong. First is rooted in preliminary screening.

A. First is strong. Logically thinking, it looks probable. Second is irrelevant and it does not have same merit.
E. First is a piece of truth. Second is a logical consequence of ban on smoking or has the same merit.

C. This is one of the rarest types of question on forcefulness of argument where the choice 'either' of the two is strong' follows. Either increase of pollution at upper level is favourable or that at ground level. So, one of these should be desirable. Hence the choice.
A. First is strong. Argument two has no substance, rejectable is preliminary screening.

A. First is strong. In fact it is established fact that a lot of resources are wasted in shifting agriculture. Second is not a strong argument at it, to beat no other methods are really more expensive in the long run than the resource or trapping shifting agriculture.
B. Second is strong as it does look probable on thinking logically. First is true but this is not reason enough to make loyalty the sole criterion for ideal citizens (no proper relation).
B. Second is strong. First statement is objectionable and debatable.

E. Both arguments are simple and straightforward. It is also true. Second is also correct, specifically they had seen in the light of the fact that the question itself suggests these are shortages for 'internal use itself'.
A. First is strong. Currently, we use exhaustible sources of energy while solar energy is not exhaustible. The second is not correct. In fact, solar energy is cheap.

B. Argument A is strong. Curbing unlawful activities of the executive is not so big a problem but no one would demand to select a solution as deducting executive pay judiciary. Second is however correct.

A. First is strong. Second is debatable.

D. None is strong. The subject of giving away a state is too sensitive a matter to be determined by joy or to get influenced by beauty or foreign countries in the joy to feel that if may settle conflicts. Suppose Kashmir was not beautiful and it did not earn any foreign exchange. Would we then have given it away?
D. None is strong. Non-violence as such is too ideological and not practical.
B. Second is strong as it is taken as universal truth. The first is a debatable point.

D. None is strong because of indirect assertions. The first may be true but why is it essential? The second says it is a wastage of resources but it also generates resources: as a result of advertisement business picks up.
D. Second is strong as it is factually correct. First is debatable. It is certainly not the 'only' way to teach discipline to employees.

E. Both are strong. It is true that the government should not promote gambling and which is done through its lotteries. It is also true that the government will not have a major source of revenue if lotteries were stopped.
B. First argument is true. Second is definitely correct.
B. Second is strong. Tax rates are decided by democratically elected ministers and they are not excessively high.
B. Only second is strong. Though many dam projects may have failed but we should see the reasons of failure instead of abandoning it fully.
D. None is strong. First is debatable. Colour T.V. may or may not be a luxury item. The second argument does not talk about colour television.
